root.title: Cúram 8.1.2 Investigations Guide
root.summary: Front matter for the Cúram 8.1.2 Investigations Guide, including the title, edition notice, contents, and legal notices. The useful content begins with an overview of Cúram Investigations: what an investigation is, how it is created as an integrated-case or standalone investigation, how ownership is assigned/transferred, and the main lifecycle steps from recording allegations and findings through resolution, approval, closure/reopening, and overriding findings on reopened cases. It also introduces investigation tools such as action plans, milestones, contact log, status history, translator need, legal actions/status, and extra tools. The section further covers configuration topics for investigation types, ownership strategies, milestones and milestone associations, resolutions, assessments, and approval checks. Pages 29–30 contain permissions, warranty/export disclaimers, privacy policy, and trademarks.

children.title: Note
children.summary: Introductory notice for the document. It advises readers to review the Notices section on page 29 before using the information or the supported product, and includes the copyright statement for Merative US L.P. (2012, 2024).

children.title: Edition
Edition — pages 5-14
children.summary: Introductory “Edition” pages for Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2 plus the start of the Cúram Investigations overview. Covers the investigation lifecycle: creating standalone or integrated-case investigations, assigning/transferring ownership, recording allegations and findings, setting resolutions, approval/rejection, closure/reopening, and overriding findings on reopened cases. Also introduces investigation participants and roles, and key investigation tools: action plans, milestones, contact log, status history, translator need, legal status/actions, and notes that ownership, milestones, and approvals are configurable.

children.title: Edition — pages 15-24
children.summary: This section covers Cúram Investigations overview topics: milestone waiver requests and approval rules; contact log use, recording/searching contacts, narrative appending and editing, and narrative history; investigation searches and extra tools like home pages, my investigations, attachments, communications, events, notes, tasks, case relationships, and ownership roles; and configuration basics for investigation types, ownership strategies, and milestones. It also introduces how investigation settings affect behavior and access.

children.title: Edition — pages 25-28
children.summary: This section explains investigation configuration in Cúram: milestone settings and how milestones are associated with investigation types, including automatic creation/completion events and component-level matching. It also covers defining investigation resolutions as selectable codes with optional workflow events, optional assessments that caseworkers can run within investigations, and investigation approval checks, including supervisor review percentages and the rule that only one active approval check can exist per investigation type.

Introductory Cúram 8.1/8.1.1/8.1.2 “Edition” pages followed by the Cúram Investigations overview. Describes the investigation lifecycle: create standalone or integrated-case investigations, assign/transfer ownership, record allegations and findings, enter resolutions, submit for approval, close/reopen, and override findings on reopened cases. Also covers participant roles and key tools such as action plans, milestones, contact log, status history, translator need, legal actions/status, and extra investigation tools. The configuration part explains investigation types, ownership strategies, milestones, resolutions, assessments, and approval checks.

children.title: Notices
Privacy policy
children.summary: This section is a legal notice page covering sample-data disclaimers, copyright license terms for sample programs, warranty/liability limits, Merative’s privacy policy link, trademark attributions for Merative and third-party brands, and the document copyright notice. It is useful for locating usage rights, privacy information, or trademark/legal references.

children.title: Trademarks
children.summary: States that all example names in the document are fictitious and that sample programs are provided by Merative “as is” under the Merative Client Agreement, with limited copying/modification/distribution rights and no warranty or liability. Includes the Merative privacy policy link. Lists trademark attributions for Merative, IBM, Adobe, Oracle/Java, Linux, Microsoft/Windows, and UNIX, and notes other names may be trademarks of their owners.

Legal notices and usage terms for the publication. Covers personal and enterprise use permissions, required preservation of proprietary notices, limits on derivative works/distribution, no implied rights beyond express permission, and Merative’s right to withdraw permission. Includes export-law compliance, “as-is” warranty disclaimer, patent notice, third-party product/site disclaimers, and copyright © Merative US L.P. 2012, 2024.

children.title: Contents
children.summary: This contents section lists the document’s front matter and chapter structure. It points to “Cúram Investigations overview” with subtopics on creating, assigning, recording allegations, entering findings and resolutions, approval, closing/reopening, roles, investigation tools, and configuration of investigation types, ownership, milestones, resolutions, assessments, and approval checks. It also includes notices, privacy policy, and trademarks. Useful as a navigation index for the investigations guide.

children.title: 1 Cúram Investigations overview
1.1 The investigation process
Creating an investigation
children.summary: Explains Cúram Investigations and how investigations are created. An investigation is an inquiry into an allegation and can be owned by users, organization groups, or work queues, with ownership transferable. It covers the investigation process from creation to closure, including recording allegations, findings, resolutions, approval, and closure, with possible reopening and override of findings. It also notes investigations can be created at the integrated case level or as standalone investigations, depending on organizational preference.

children.title: Assigning investigation ownership
children.summary: Explains how investigation ownership is assigned and managed. Standalone investigations default to the primary client’s administrator as owner, while investigations created from an integrated case default to the case owner. Ownership can be transferred to a user or organization group such as an organization unit, position, or work queue, allowing members to work the investigation. Notes that agencies can override the sample ownership strategy through configuration. Also briefly introduces that investigations must record allegations, which become fixed once submitted for approval, approved, or closed.

children.title: Recording allegations for an investigation
children.summary: This section explains how allegations are recorded when creating an investigation. It notes that investigations may have one or more allegations describing the reported issue, source, participants, roles, location, dates, and other details. Mandatory fields include allegation type and date. It also states that allegations become read-only once an investigation is submitted for approval, approved, or closed, so they cannot be added, deleted, or modified in those states.

children.title: Entering allegation findings
children.summary: Explains how to enter allegation findings on an investigation and how they drive resolution and approval. Findings such as Substantiated, Unsubstantiated, and Indicated are recorded per allegation; all allegations need findings before the investigation can be resolved. Findings cannot be changed once an investigation is submitted for approval, approved, or closed. The section also covers selecting an overall investigation resolution from configured options, related workflow events, and the approval step where a supervisor reviews, approves, or rejects the recorded findings and resolution.

children.title: Entering the investigation resolution
children.summary: Explains how to resolve an investigation after allegation findings are entered. Each allegation needs a recorded finding (for example, Substantiated, Unsubstantiated, Indicated), and findings can’t be changed once the investigation is submitted for approval, approved, or closed. The overall investigation resolution is then recorded based on the findings, using configured resolution codes that may trigger workflow events. Finally, the investigation is submitted for supervisor approval, where it may be approved or rejected and returned for more work.

children.title: Approving the investigation
children.summary: This section explains that once a resolution is recorded, an investigation must be submitted for approval. A supervisor reviews the allegations, findings, and overall resolution to confirm they are correct. The investigation may be manually approved if appropriate, or rejected and returned to the user if changes or additional work are needed. It also notes that findings cannot be modified after submission, approval, or closure.

children.title: Closing or reopening an investigation
children.summary: Explains when an investigation can be closed or reopened. An investigation may be closed at any stage if further organization involvement is unnecessary, typically after allegations are resolved, findings need no further action, and the investigation is approved. Closure requires a reason, and closure details can later be changed. A closed investigation may be reopened for reasons such as an incorrect decision, new information, or error, with a reopening reason recorded. Also notes that findings on reopened investigations can be overridden with a reason and effective date, with finding history retained.

children.title: Overriding a finding on a reopened investigation
children.summary: Explains that if a closed investigation is reopened, an existing allegation finding can be overridden when the original finding is incorrect. To change it, the user must enter the reason for the change and the effective date of the new finding. A finding history is kept for all allegation findings, including effective dates and override reasons.

children.title: Summary of participant roles played in an investigation
children.summary: Describes investigation participants and their roles in an investigation. Participants may be added manually or automatically, and additional people not present at creation can be added later during the case. The section sits after approval, closure/reopening, and finding override procedures, and introduces how direct and indirect roles are tracked in the investigation record.

This section outlines the end-to-end investigation process: creating an investigation at integrated case or standalone level, assigning and transferring ownership, recording one or more allegations, entering allegation findings, recording the overall resolution, obtaining approval, and closing or reopening the investigation. It also covers restrictions on modifying allegations/findings after submission, approval-check behavior, closure reasons, reopening reasons, and overriding findings on reopened investigations with history tracking.

children.title: 1.2 Tools for conducting an investigation
Monitoring the investigation action plan
children.summary: Explains investigation action plans: they identify actions needed to address concerns for each participant during an investigation, linking situations, related allegations, expected/actual dates, and responsible case participants or users. Situations can be recorded with an action or later associated with one. The section sits within an overview of investigation tools, which also cover milestones, contact logs, status history, translators, legal actions, and standard tools.

children.title: Tracking milestones
Milestone Waiver Request Approval
children.summary: Explains how milestone waiver requests work for investigation milestones. Automatic milestones may need a waiver to change expected start/end dates; manually created milestones can be changed directly. Describes configuration settings that control whether waivers can be submitted and whether approval is required, plus approval check precedence across user, organization unit, and milestone levels. Covers the workflow: submitted, automatic approval if not required, otherwise notification, approved/rejected status, and date changes taking effect. Includes a brief segue into using the contact log.

Explains investigation milestones: they track significant events/tasks, can be owned by someone other than the investigation owner, and may be created manually or automatically from configured milestone definitions. Manual milestones use expected/actual start and end dates, with workflow notifications if dates are missed. Automatic milestones can be created and closed when investigation events occur, though development is required to enable this. Also notes milestones are configured in investigation administration and can be used across investigations.

children.title: Using the contact log
children.summary: This section explains how to use the contact log to record, search, edit, and review investigation contacts. It covers capturing contact details, participants, attachments, and required narratives; appending narrative addenda and viewing narrative history; editing narratives within a configurable editable period; and searching contacts by participant, location, purpose, method, date range, and text in the narrative/subject. It also notes display limits, preview/PDF print options, and that only the latest contact details are tracked for updates, not full history.

children.title: Viewing the investigation status history
children.summary: Explains that every investigation has an automatically maintained status history showing details of each status change over its lifetime. The history updates when a user submits an investigation for approval, approves it, or rejects it. Useful for viewing investigation lifecycle changes and approval workflow events.

children.title: Determining the need for a translator
children.summary: Explains when an investigation needs a translator. Translation may be required if case workers cannot communicate with the client in the client’s preferred language, which is recorded at registration. The need can be set manually by a caseworker or determined automatically by the system, and it is evaluated per investigation rather than globally for the client. Example given: a Spanish-speaking client may need a translator if the worker cannot speak Spanish.

children.title: Managing legal actions and legal status
children.summary: Explains how caseworkers record legal actions and legal status during an investigation. Legal actions support directives, hearings, petitions, and orders from legal authorities, including actions that lead to other legal actions. Three supported categories are Legal Petition, Legal Hearing, and Legal Order. It also covers tracking a participant’s legal status over time, with examples like adjudicated, crown ward, temporary custody, and parental rights terminated. Legal actions/status types are configured in administration.

children.title: Extra tools for managing an investigation
Viewing my investigations
children.summary: This section describes investigation navigation views for users. It covers the Investigator home page summary (today’s appointments, due/overdue tasks, items of interest, assigned investigations, and pending approvals), the My Investigations view with configurable ownership filters plus type/status filtering, investigation query results with saved personal queries and criteria such as client, type, ownership, status, and time period, and views for recently approved and recently assigned investigations.

children.title: Searching for investigations
children.summary: This section explains how users find and access investigations. It covers recently viewed investigations and items of interest for quick return access, then details investigation search criteria: reference number, client name, client reference number, type, subtype, status, and start/end date filters. It also introduces attachments and communications, including supported file types, access/security, CMS integration, and communication creation via templates or email.

children.title: Adding attachments
children.summary: Describes adding attachments to an investigation: attaching supplemental files such as scanned documents, transcripts, bank statements, certificates, invoices, and pay slips. Notes supported formats include Word, Excel, and PDF, and that attached files are accessible only to users with appropriate security privileges. Also mentions optional integration with a content management system for storage/retrieval. Important: attachments are also maintained for product delivery cases, integrated cases, and participants.

children.title: Maintaining communications
children.summary: Explains investigation-related communication handling. A communication is correspondence to or from the organization and can be integrated with Microsoft Word templates, XSL templates, or email servers. Communications may be paper, telephone, or email based. Any communication created from an investigation’s communication list page is automatically linked to that investigation, and the correspondent is assigned the investigation participant role of correspondent.

children.title: Tracking investigation events
children.summary: Explains how investigation events, notes, tasks, and case relationships are tracked in Cúram Investigations. Covers system- and user-created events, including an events calendar and investigation closure events. Describes entering, prioritizing, sensitizing, updating, and history tracking for notes. Summarizes how investigation tasks are created manually or automatically and managed in a user inbox or case task list. Also explains creating relationships between cases, manually or during processing.

children.title: Entering and updating notes
children.summary: Explains how users add and manage investigation notes. Notes can be free text, prioritized, and assigned sensitivity so only certain users can access them. Users can update priority, sensitivity, and append text; if configured, original text can be edited for a limited time. The system keeps note history with each version, timestamps, and the modifying user. Also points to related topics on note configuration and note editing/appending in other guides.

children.title: Using tasks to manage work on investigations
children.summary: Explains how investigation work is managed through tasks in an investigation case’s task list. Defines tasks as instructions for work, created manually or by the system, and maintained in a user’s workspace/workflow. Gives an example of an approval task appearing in both the user’s inbox and the case task list. Also notes related investigation features on the page: tracking events, entering/updating notes, and recording case relationships.

children.title: Recording case relationships
children.summary: Explains how investigation case relationships are recorded. Users can create links between one case and another, such as connecting an investigation to a related product delivery case involving the same client. Relationships may be created manually or automatically during investigation processing.

children.title: Assigning user roles
children.summary: This section explains how investigation ownership and user roles are assigned. It says ownership can be given to a user, organization unit, position, or work queue, and standard user role functions record the owner and supervisor so users in that structure can act on the investigation. It also introduces investigation configuration: types, milestones, and resolutions must be set up first, and investigation types can define home page, workflow events, security rights, translation requirements, and ownership strategy.

This section covers extra tools for managing investigations, including investigator home page summaries, My Investigations views with ownership/type/status filters, saved investigation queries, recently approved/assigned/viewed investigations, and items of interest. It also explains searching by reference, client, subtype, status, and dates; adding attachments and communications; tracking events; entering/updating notes with history and sensitivity; using tasks; and recording case relationships.

Overview of investigation tools: action plans for documenting required actions, related allegations, dates, and responsible participants/users; milestones for tracking significant events, with manual or automatic creation, ownership, deadlines, and waiver approval; contact logs for recording, searching, editing, and printing investigation contacts and narratives; status history for lifecycle/approval changes; translator need based on client language vs worker skills; legal actions/status tracking; and extra tools such as investigator views, queries, attachments, communications, events, notes, tasks, and case relationships.

children.title: 1.3 Configuring investigations
Defining investigation types
children.summary: This section explains how investigation types are defined and configured in Cúram. It covers configurable investigation metadata such as home page, start/close workflow events, security rights, translation requirements, ownership strategy, and whether only case members can be selected as the concerning participant in contact logs. It also notes that investigations can inherit preconfigured information at creation and that ownership can be assigned to users, organization units, positions, or work queues.

children.title: Configuring investigation ownership
children.summary: This section explains how investigation ownership is configured for investigation types. It says ownership can be assigned to a user, organization unit, position, or work queue, using standard user role functions to record the owner and supervisor. It also describes the Investigation Ownership Strategy setting, which lets administrators define workflow-based rules for determining the initial investigation owner, with the option to override the default strategy for different organizational needs.

children.title: Configuring investigation milestones
children.summary: This section explains how investigation milestones are configured through milestone configuration records. It covers required settings such as Name and Type, Earliest Start Day, Duration, and Start Date, plus optional End Date, Expected Date Extension Allowed, Waiver Required, and Milestone Added workflow event. It also explains how these settings control automatic and manual milestone expected start/end dates, milestone availability periods, and whether dates can be changed or require waiver approval.

children.title: Associating milestones with investigations
children.summary: Explains how administrators associate milestone configurations with investigation types to support manual and automatic milestone creation. A new milestone can be defined during association or an existing one selected. For automatic milestones, creation and completion workflow events trigger and close milestone instances, e.g. Approve Investigation and Close Investigation. Matching can be refined by component type and category, distinguishing investigation-level from component-level milestones such as Action Plan.

children.title: Defining investigation resolutions
children.summary: This section explains how to define investigation resolutions for an investigation type, including selectable outcome codes such as Founded and Unfounded, and optional workflow events triggered when a resolution is entered. It also notes that organizations can optionally assign predefined assessments to investigation types to support resolution decisions. Finally, it covers configuring investigation approval checks, where supervisors review a percentage of investigations, reject incorrect findings or resolutions, and only one active approval check can exist per investigation type.

children.title: Setting up assessments for investigation types
children.summary: This section explains how to configure investigation-type settings in system administration: assigning predefined assessments, defining investigation resolutions, and setting up approval checks. It notes that assessments are optional and not provided by default, but can be enabled for caseworkers to run during investigations. It also covers resolution codes like Founded/Unfounded, their possible workflow events, and supervisor approval checks, including configurable approval percentages and the rule that only one active approval check can exist per investigation type.

children.title: Configuring investigation approval checks
children.summary: Describes optional investigation approval checks for each investigation type. A supervisor can review allegations, findings, and overall resolution to prevent incorrect or erroneous investigation outcomes. Administrators configure what percentage of investigations require manual approval, such as 50% being routed to a supervisor. Supervisors can reject investigations if they disagree with findings or resolution. Only one active approval check can exist at a time for a given investigation type.

This section explains how to configure investigations in Cúram so case-level investigations can be created with inherited setup. It covers defining investigation types (home page, workflow events, security rights, translation, ownership strategy, and contact-log participant limits), assigning ownership to users, org units, positions, or work queues, configuring milestone rules and associations for manual or automatic milestones, and defining resolutions, optional assessments, and supervisor approval checks.

Overview of Cúram Investigations: defines investigations as inquiries into allegations, created for integrated cases or as standalone investigations, with transferable ownership by user, organization unit, position, or work queue. Explains the full process from recording allegations and findings to setting an overall resolution, approval, closure, reopening, and overriding findings on reopened cases. Also introduces investigation tools and administrative configuration for ownership, milestones, resolutions, assessments, and approval checks. Subsequent pages include notices and copyright material.
